scenarios.name: ws-new-drop-reuse
scenarios.desc: waitable-set index allocation is LIFO free-list reuse: new(1), new(2), drop(1), new -> reuses 1.

scenarios.name: poll-empty-none
scenarios.desc: waitable-set.poll on a set with nothing joined returns (NONE,0,0) without blocking.

scenarios.name: join-rejoin-leave
scenarios.desc: a subtask joins set A, rejoins set B (implicit leave of A), then leaves via join(_,0); final table pins the still-unresolved subtask.

scenarios.name: import-immediate
scenarios.desc: resolve_after:0 resolves inside the call itself -> ret [RETURNED] (2), no table entry; the result is still delivered through the retptr.

scenarios.name: import-deferred-wait
scenarios.desc: one deferred async import (resolve_after:1), joined to a set, waited on -> SUBTASK event, retptr result observed via mem.check, subtask+set dropped, task.return.

scenarios.name: import-two-fifo
scenarios.desc: two deferred imports (after:1, after:2) joined to ONE set -> wait delivers A then B, in (after, registration-order) order.

scenarios.name: import-two-sets-poll
scenarios.desc: A and B in DIFFERENT sets: waiting on B's set pays enough rounds to also resolve A (globally ordered), so a later poll on A's set observes it with no extra round.

scenarios.name: double-task-return
scenarios.desc: a second task.return on an already-resolved task traps.

scenarios.name: task-cancel-undelivered
scenarios.desc: task.cancel with no cancellation ever delivered traps.

scenarios.name: host-cancel-blocking-wait
scenarios.desc: a cancellable wait on an empty set + host.cancel-root(after:1) delivers TASK_CANCELLED synchronously (the task's one thread is cancellable and resumable); task.cancel then resolves it.

scenarios.name: host-cancel-uncancellable-then-poll
scenarios.desc: a non-cancellable wait absorbs the cancellation request as PENDING_CANCEL (no cancellable thread to resume); a later cancellable poll delivers TASK_CANCELLED via the deliver_pending_cancel prologue, without ever driving the scheduler again.

scenarios.name: wait-empty-deadlock
scenarios.desc: waiting on an empty set with nothing queued can never become ready -> normalized deadlock trap (design doc §2.3).

scenarios.name: ws-drop-nonempty
scenarios.desc: dropping a waitable-set with a joined (unresolved) subtask still in it traps.

scenarios.name: wrong-handle-kind-wait
scenarios.desc: waitable-set.wait on a handle that names a subtask (not a waitable set) traps.

scenarios.name: join-set-as-waitable-traps
scenarios.desc: waitable.join with a waitable-set handle passed as the WAITABLE argument traps (a waitable-set is not itself a Waitable).

scenarios.name: drop-stale-index-traps
scenarios.desc: dropping a handle index that was already removed traps (the table no longer has any entry, of any kind, at that index).

scenarios.name: subtask-drop-unresolved
scenarios.desc: dropping a subtask that never resolved (behavior:never) traps: resolve was never delivered.

scenarios.name: subtask-cancel-async-blocked
scenarios.desc: behavior:ignore-cancel (resolve_after:2): an async cancel returns BLOCKED (the callee ignores cancellation), and the subtask still resolves normally at its scheduled round -- completion wins.

scenarios.name: subtask-cancel-sync-cancelled
scenarios.desc: behavior:cancel-resolves-cancelled: a synchronous cancel resolves the subtask as CANCELLED_BEFORE_RETURNED (4) immediately (onCancel calls ResolveCancelled synchronously).

scenarios.name: subtask-cancel-racing-completion
scenarios.desc: import A (after:1) resolves while the guest is paying rounds waiting on a DIFFERENT import B's set; cancelling A afterward hits the resolved-but-undelivered fast path -- ret [RETURNED] (2), never BLOCKED.

scenarios.name: subtask-cancel-twice
scenarios.desc: cancelling the same subtask twice traps the second time (cancellation_requested).

scenarios.name: stream-rendezvous
scenarios.desc: an async write parks (BLOCKED), a sync read on the other end completes the rendezvous immediately; the writer's own COMPLETED event is retrieved later via join+wait; both ends dropped.

scenarios.name: stream-zero-length-write-handshake
scenarios.desc: a zero-length async read parks first; a zero-length sync write against it COMPLETEs the write WITHOUT waking the parked reader (the write-side zero-length elif, stream.go's documented asymmetry) -- final table pins the reader still COPYING and the writer IDLE.

scenarios.name: stream-drop-writable-then-read-dropped
scenarios.desc: dropping the writable end while idle, then a read against the (now dropped) shared stream returns DROPPED.

scenarios.name: stream-drop-readable-while-copying-traps
scenarios.desc: dropping a stream end with an in-flight (parked) copy traps.

scenarios.name: stream-cancel-read
scenarios.desc: an async read parks (BLOCKED), then a synchronous cancel-read completes it as CANCELLED (packed 2|0<<4); final table pins the end back to idle.

scenarios.name: future-once
scenarios.desc: a future's single-element rendezvous: async write parks, sync read completes it with the value; the writer's COMPLETED event is retrieved via join+wait; a SECOND write on the (now DONE, not idle) writable end traps.

scenarios.name: misc-context-ec
scenarios.desc: context.get/set slot round-trip plus error-context new/drop index-allocation coverage (interleaved with other kinds elsewhere); messages are deliberately empty since DETERMINISTIC_PROFILE forces the reference's error-context content to '' while wazy always loads the real bytes -- content itself is NOT part of this oracle's diffed surface (see the design doc's honest-scope notes), so every message here is zero-length to keep both sides trivially equal.

scenarios.name: backpressure-underflow-traps
scenarios.desc: backpressure.dec below zero traps.
